title: Challenge Lab: Secure Secret Management with AWS Secrets Manager and ECS

steps.text: Click on the Open Console button, and you will get redirected to AWS Console in a new browser tab.

On the AWS sign-in page, Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab. Now copy your Username and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab.
Now copy your Username and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Once Signed in to the AWS Management Console, Make the default AWS Region as US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1.
Sign in to AWS Portal

XYZ Corporation needs to securely manage sensitive data in their containerized applications. In this challenge, you will store a secret in AWS Secrets Manager, reference it in an ECS task definition, deploy the application, and verify that the container securely accesses the secret. XYZ Corporation needs to securely manage sensitive data in their containerized applications. In this lab, you will store a secret in AWS Secrets Manager, reference it in an ECS task, deploy the application, and verify that the container can access the secret securely.

Create a Secrets Manager secret Secret Type: Select Other type of secret Value Format: Choose Plaintext, then enter password_value Encryption Key: Use the default key Secret Name: ProductionUserCredentials
Secret Type: Select Other type of secret
Value Format: Choose Plaintext, then enter password_value
Encryption Key: Use the default key
Secret Name: ProductionUserCredentials
Copy the ARN of IAM role ecsTaskExecutionRole (pre-configured)
Create a security group with SSH inbound rules for ECS cluster instances.
Create a Key Pair for the EC2 instance present inside ECS Cluster

Create an ECS Cluster Cluster Name: whiz ECS Cluster Type: Select Amazon EC2 Set Provisioning Model to On-Demand. Instance Type: t2.micro Subnets: us-east-1a, us-east-1b SSH Key Pair: <key-pair> Security Group: ECS-SG Root EBS Volume Size: 30 GiB Launch the ECS cluster.
Cluster Name: whiz
ECS Cluster Type: Select Amazon EC2
Set Provisioning Model to On-Demand.
Instance Type: t2.micro
Subnets: us-east-1a, us-east-1b
SSH Key Pair: <key-pair>
Security Group: ECS-SG
Root EBS Volume Size: 30 GiB
Launch the ECS cluster.

Create a task definition Create new Task Definition and choose the option to create with JSON. Set executionRoleArn with your IAM role ARN to allow ECS task access to AWS services. Define containerDefinitions with container settings: Use entryPoint to run shell commands (sh -c). Map port 80 on host to port 80 in container (TCP) for web traffic. Specify command to create a simple HTML page and start Apache server. Allocate CPU (10 units) and memory (300 MB) for the container. Inject secrets via secrets, using your secret ARN as valueFrom and environment variable name (e.g., username_value). Use the Docker image httpd:2.4 (Apache HTTP Server). Mark container as essential to keep the task running. Name the container (e.g., ecs-secrets-container). Set family name (e.g., ecs-secrets-tutorial) to group task revisions.
Create new Task Definition and choose the option to create with JSON.
Set executionRoleArn with your IAM role ARN to allow ECS task access to AWS services.

Define containerDefinitions with container settings: Use entryPoint to run shell commands (sh -c). Map port 80 on host to port 80 in container (TCP) for web traffic. Specify command to create a simple HTML page and start Apache server. Allocate CPU (10 units) and memory (300 MB) for the container. Inject secrets via secrets, using your secret ARN as valueFrom and environment variable name (e.g., username_value). Use the Docker image httpd:2.4 (Apache HTTP Server). Mark container as essential to keep the task running. Name the container (e.g., ecs-secrets-container). Set family name (e.g., ecs-secrets-tutorial) to group task revisions.
Use entryPoint to run shell commands (sh -c).
Map port 80 on host to port 80 in container (TCP) for web traffic.
Specify command to create a simple HTML page and start Apache server.
Allocate CPU (10 units) and memory (300 MB) for the container.
Inject secrets via secrets, using your secret ARN as valueFrom and environment variable name (e.g., username_value).
Use the Docker image httpd:2.4 (Apache HTTP Server).
Mark container as essential to keep the task running.
Name the container (e.g., ecs-secrets-container).
Set family name (e.g., ecs-secrets-tutorial) to group task revisions.
Start the task Cluster: ECS cluster configured in above steps Launch Type: EC2 Tags: Disable ECS managed tags
Cluster: ECS cluster configured in above steps
Launch Type: EC2
Tags: Disable ECS managed tags
SSH into EC2 Instance

List Docker Processes and Print Secret List all running Docker containers using Docker commands to identify the target container. Copy the container ID of ecs-secrets-container. Execute a command inside the container using the copied container ID. Connect to the container using its ID. Use the echo command to check the value of the username_value environment variable. Verify that the output displays password_value, confirming the task is successful.
List all running Docker containers using Docker commands to identify the target container.
Copy the container ID of ecs-secrets-container.
Execute a command inside the container using the copied container ID.
Connect to the container using its ID.
Use the echo command to check the value of the username_value environment variable.
Verify that the output displays password_value, confirming the task is successful.
Click on the Validate to complete the challenge.
Sign out of the AWS Account
You have successfully completed the lab.
Once you have completed the steps, click on End Lab from the lab console.
